attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of left iliac fossa pain, constipation, and intermittent fever. She had diarrhoea two days ago, with no mucous or blood passed. She denies urinary symptoms, weight loss, and vomiting. She reports having a similar episode one year ago, which resolved in one day. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. She describes a diet high in red meat and low in fruits and vegetables. She has a family history of bowel cancer in her aunt (diagnosed at 65 years of age).

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.1°C. Her mucous membranes are moist. There is mild abdominal distention with tenderness localised to the left iliac fossa, where a palpable mass is felt. No rebound tenderness is elicited.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with left iliac fossa pain, fever, and altered bowel habits, which are classic features of diverticulitis. The presence of a palpable mass in the left iliac fossa further supports this diagnosis. Risk factors include her age, low-fibre diet, and previous similar episode.

Acute diverticulitis is the most likely diagnosis given the clinical presentation. Diverticulitis occurs when diverticula (outpouchings of the colonic mucosa) become inflamed or infected. The sigmoid colon is the most common site, explaining the left-sided pain. The palpable mass likely represents inflamed bowel with surrounding inflammation or a phlegmon. The history of a similar self-resolving episode is also consistent with diverticulitis, which can have recurrent episodes.

Sigmoid volvulus typically presents with more severe obstruction symptoms including significant abdominal distention, absolute constipation, and inability to pass flatus. Patients are often more unwell with signs of bowel ischaemia. The presence of diarrhoea two days ago makes complete obstruction less likely.

Colorectal cancer remains in the differential diagnosis but is less likely given the acute presentation with fever. Colorectal cancer typically presents with more insidious symptoms such as change in bowel habit, weight loss, or rectal bleeding over weeks to months. While her family history puts her at slightly increased risk, the acute nature of her symptoms make cancer less likely than diverticulitis.

Ulcerative colitis typically presents with bloody diarrhoea, tenesmus, and urgency. The absence of blood or mucus in the stool makes this diagnosis less likely. Additionally, ulcerative colitis more commonly presents in younger individuals (15-45 years), although it can occur at any age.

Acute appendicitis classically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to the right iliac fossa, associated with anorexia and vomiting. The left-sided location of this patient's pain makes appendicitis highly unlikely, as the appendix is located in the right lower quadrant.
learningPoints: Diverticulitis typically presents with left iliac fossa pain, fever, and altered bowel habits in older adults.
Risk factors for diverticulitis include low-fibre diet, increased age, and previous episodes.
A palpable mass in the left iliac fossa with localised tenderness is a characteristic finding in acute diverticulitis.
references: Smith JA, Kaye AH, Christophi C, Brown WA. Textbook of surgery. 4th ed. Hoboken (NJ): Wiley Global Research (STMS); 2019.

Crowe FL, Appleby PN, Allen NE, Key TJ. Diet and risk of diverticular disease in Oxford cohort of European Prospective Investigation into Cancer and Nutrition (EPIC): prospective study of British vegetarians and non-vegetarians. BMJ. 2011;343:d4131. Available from: https://www.bmj.com/content/343/bmj.d4131
